english: pf. and imp. ton (thon), vb.n. for odón-pa ('don pa), in W. very frq., in B. less so, = byúṅ-ba (byung ba), 1. to come out, to go out, *dáṅ - sa kàṅ - pa - ne ton* (dang - sa khang - pa - ne thon) he is just coming out of the house; kun p̱yir otón-te (kun phyir 'thon te) all coming out Mil.: to remove (from a house or place), to leave, *ton-čúg* (thon cug) W. the last farewell; to depart, to emigrate; ču p̱á-gar tón-nas (chu pha gar thon nas) when I shall be beyond the river Mil.; more carelessly: *yul tón - na, lúṅ-pa tón-na* (yul thon - na, lung pa thon na) W. when one has passed through, the village, the valley; *dún-du tón - če* (dun du thon - ce) to step or come forth (from the crowd etc.); to rise, arise, originate, v. snyíṅ-rús (snying rus). — 2. for oṅ-ba ('ong ba), to come, esp. Bal. — 3. to come from, to proceed from, to have origin, bod kō - ràṅ-nas tón-pa yín (bod ko - rang nas thon pa yin) these are products of Tibet itself; hence: to occur, like oṅ-ba ('ong ba), tsóṅ-pas kúr-nas oton dug (tsong pas khur nas 'thon dug) (these goods) occur as imported, are imported; ríg-pa-čan miṅ oton yín-te (rig pa can ming 'thon yin te) known as being acute, sagacious.

1. the letter d, originally, and in the frontier districts also at present, pronounced like the German d, i.e. not quite so soft as the English d; in C. as initial aspirated and low-toned, dh; as final letter half dropped, and changing a preceding a, o, u into ạ, ọ, ụ; as prefix in Kh. and Bal. = γ, not differing from the prefixed g. — da-drág (da drag) is a term used by grammarians, for the now obsolete d as second final, after n, r, l, e.g. in kund (kund), changing the termination du (du) into tu (tu); no (no), ro (ro), lo (lo) into to (to); nam (nam), ram (ram), lam (lam) into tam (tam). — 2. num. figure for 11.

to be dim, dull, clouded, of the senses and the understanding, *nyid tóm-če* (nyid thom ce) W. to slumber, to doze, *nyid yúr-če* (nyid yur ce) id.; mgo-(bo) otóm (mgo-(bo) 'thom) consciousness is clouded or darkened, by intoxication, disease Med.; also of religious darkness Pth.; *mig tom - tóm ča dug* (mig thom - thom ca dug) W. he is dazzled (by the brightness of the sun); ldoṅs-šiṅ otóms-par gyur (ldongs shing 'thoms par gyur) having become blind Dzl.
fragment, of a book Tar., cf. tór-bu (thor bu).

pf. btor (btor), fut. γtor (gtor), imp. otór ('thor), 1. prop. vb.n. to be scattered, of leaves by the wind Dzl., to fly asunder, to be dispersed; to fall to pieces, to decay, of the body after death Mil.; to burst, of a gun; but also vb.a.: mé-tog otór-ba (me tog 'thor ba) to strew flowers Glr., Dzl.; tor-tuṅ (thor thung) libation Cs., ču-otór (chu 'thor) libation of water Sch.; cf. γtór-ba (gtor ba). — 2. W.: to have notches, flaws, of edge-tools.

1. gen. at the head of a sentence: now, at present, just, esp. before the imp. mood: da kar-dáṅ-la soṅ (da kar dang la song) just go to Kardang! directly, immediately, forthwith, instantly; in narration sometimes (though rarely) for then, at that time. — 2. in col. language after the emphatical word of the sentence: it is true, to be sure, indeed, *loṅ da yod ṅul med* (long da yod ngul med) time I have, it is true, but no money. Comp. da-ko (da ko) Sch. = da (da). — dá-či (da ci) a little while ago, lately. Mil. and col. — dá-ča (da ca) in future, henceforward. — da-nyid (da nyid) the present time; but just now. — dá-lta(r) (da lta(r))
